claim: Under the bilateral Memorandum of Understanding negotiated in 2016 and running through fiscal year 2028, the United States has committed to providing Israel with $38 billion in military assistance — $33 billion in Foreign Military Financing grants plus $5 billion in missile-defense appropriations — at $3.8 billion per year. In April 2024, Congress passed legislation including an additional $3.5 billion in Foreign Military Financing and $5.2 billion in defense appropriations (with $4 billion for missile defense and $1.2 billion for the Iron Beam laser defense system). In early January 2025, the administration notified Congress of additional possible munitions sales to Israel totaling approximately $8 billion. The Congressional Research Service publishes a regularly-updated overview (RL33222) as the authoritative U.S. legislative reference.

sources.label: Congressional Research Service — “U.S. Foreign Aid to Israel: Overview and Developments since October 7, 2023” (RL33222)
Congressional Research Service — RL33222 PDF (regularly updated)
Council on Foreign Relations — “U.S. Aid to Israel in Four Charts”
sources.quote: U.S. Aid to Israel in Four Charts

sources.label: Watson Institute (Brown University), Costs of War — “U.S. Military Aid and Arms Transfers to Israel, October 2023” (Hartung)
James Li / @5149jamesli, Instagram reel DVb9cgWEt8D (Mar 3, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: These figures are the authoritative U.S. legislative record. Theo Von's editorial framing — that the U.S. is functionally a vassal of Israel via this aid — is the contested interpretation. The numerical commitments themselves are public, audited, and uncontested. The CRS report is the primary citation; the Costs of War accounting from Brown's Watson Institute provides an independent tracking of supplementals and arms transfers.
